message:
perf(strchrnul): single-pass scan instead of strchr+strlen on miss

`strchrnul(s, c)` is the GNU extension that returns the index of the
first `c` in `s` or — if `c` is not found — the index of the
terminating NUL (i.e. effectively `strlen(s)`).

The previous implementation in `crates/frankenlibc-core/src/string/str.rs`
chained two passes:

```rust
strchr(s, c).unwrap_or_else(|| strlen(s))
```

That walks `s` once looking for `c`, and on the (very common) miss
path walks it a *second* time looking for `0`. For long strings
without a hit, this doubles the work for no semantic gain — the
scanning loop already has every byte in cache and can check both
sentinels at once.

Replace it with a single inline scan that returns the first index where
the byte is either `c` or `0`, falling back to `s.len()` if neither
appears (matching the previous fallback exactly when `s` is not
NUL-terminated within its slice — the GNU contract is "either the
first c or the terminating NUL", and `s.len()` only matters for the
malformed input case which we keep behaviorally identical).

Bench (crates/frankenlibc-bench/benches/string_bench.rs):

* New `bench_strchrnul_absent`: builds an `'A' * size` buffer with a
  trailing NUL for sizes 16/64/256/1024/4096 and searches for `'Z'`
  (the absent / miss path — the case the optimization actually
  speeds up). Uses the same `iter_custom` + `BenchStats` reporting
  pattern as the surrounding benches and is registered alongside
  `bench_memcpy_sizes`, `bench_strlen`, `bench_memcmp_sizes`,
  `bench_strcmp` in the criterion group.

`strchrnul` is also re-exported into the `string_bench` use list.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
